Fort Stanwix was a fort built during the colonial era in 1758 under the command of British General John Stanwix, located in present-day Rome, New York. Although construction began on August 26, 1758, it was not completed until four years later in 1762. Initially, it served as a strategic outpost during the French and Indian War. The fort played a significant role in the American Revolutionary War as well, particularly during the Saratoga campaign. It was here that the British General Barry St. Leger was forced to surrender in 1777, a pivotal moment that convinced France to formally ally with the American colonies. The fort was eventually abandoned and fell into disrepair, but its historical significance led to its preservation. Today, Fort Stanwix National Monument stands as a testament to this critical period in American history, offering visitors a glimpse into the military strategies and daily life of the 18th century. The site includes reconstructed earthworks, a museum, and a memorial to the Oneida people, who were crucial allies of the Continental Army.
